title: The Owner-Dependency Discount: What Buyers Price In Before They Make You an Offer
linkedin:
The gap between what an owner expects to sell for and what a buyer actually offers is rarely about the financials.

A business generating $600,000 EBITDA at a 6x multiple is worth $3.6 million. The same business, same earnings, carrying heavy owner dependency, might attract a 4x offer. That $1.2 million gap doesn't close in the negotiation. It closes in the years before the sale process starts.

Buyers price concentration as risk. When key clients call the owner directly, when decisions stall without the owner present, when six weeks of unreachable leave would visibly slow the business, experienced acquirers translate that into a lower multiple before the first offer lands.

Due diligence questions that sound routine, including how the owner spends their time and which calls require their involvement, are actually the buyer building their key person discount.

Three levers reduce owner dependency: relationship transfer (bringing a second person into key client relationships so clients connect with the business, not just with you), decision architecture (defining what your team can decide without you, then enforcing that autonomy consistently), and process documentation (clean notes for the fifteen things that matter most, not lengthy manuals).

This work takes 12 to 18 months to show up in the track record buyers need to see. For owners planning an exit in the next two to four years, June is a useful point to start the clock.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#BusinessValuation #SMEFinance #AustralianBusiness
facebook:
When a buyer's offer comes back lower than expected on a profitable business, owner dependency is often the reason, and it's rarely visible from the inside.

Buyers price the risk that key client relationships, decisions, and operational knowledge live with the owner personally rather than with the business itself. That risk shows up in the multiple before any negotiation starts.

What would happen to your business if you took six weeks off, completely unreachable?

The full post breaks down what buyers are actually assessing and the three steps that shift the number before the sale process begins.

google:
If you're planning to sell your business in the next two to four years, the EBITDA number isn't the only figure that matters to a buyer.

Owner dependency is one of the most common reasons a sale price comes back lower than expected. When key clients deal primarily with the owner, when decisions slow without the owner present, and when operational knowledge sits in the owner's head rather than in documented business systems, buyers price that concentration as risk and reduce the purchase multiple accordingly.

On a business generating $600,000 EBITDA, the difference between a 4x and a 6x multiple is $1.2 million. That gap rarely closes in negotiation. It closes in the two to three years of structured preparation before the sale process starts.

For owner-led businesses across Queensland, NSW, and Victoria, June is a practical point to assess where you stand. The work of reducing owner dependency takes 12 to 18 months to register in the track record buyers actually assess during due diligence.

The latest ProfitPulse post covers what buyers look for, the questions they ask during due diligence, and the three practical levers that move the number before you're in the room.

Read the full post here: https://profit-pulse.com.au/blog-post
